title: Chapter 23: The Standoff
content: The Primordial Council responded to Roxanne's refusal by declaring her a rogue actor.
They sent a formal declaration through the Nether-Grid Exchange: the Second Seat was stripped of her position. Her Sovereign Account was frozen. Her territory was marked for reclamation.

Roxanne didn't care. She'd spent the past two days buying every debt contract she could find. Student loans. Mortgages. Soul contracts. She bought them all and forgave them. Thousands of people across the city — humans who had been trapped in Nether-Grid debt — suddenly found themselves free.
The Primordial Council's enforcement arm arrived at noon.
It was bigger than the last one. Twelve Enforcement units, led by a Praetor — a being of solidified law, its body made of inscribed stone tablets, its voice the sound of a gavel striking marble.

"Roxanne Vance," the Praetor intoned. "By order of the Primordial Council, you are charged with: unauthorized use of a Primordial artifact, interference with Nether-Grid economic operations, and incitement of debtors. Surrender the card and submit to judgment."
Roxanne stood at the main gate. Silas beside her. The Executioner behind her. The two Enforcement units she'd bought flanking her.
"I have a counter-offer."
"No further negotiations are authorized."
"I wasn't negotiating. I was informing."
She raised the card.
*PURCHASE: PRIMORDIAL COUNCIL ENFORCEMENT DIVISION — COMPLETE BUYOUT. PRICE: CALCULATING...*
The card pulsed. Once. Twice. Three times.
*PRICE: 500,000,000 OBOLS.*
*APPROVED.*
The twelve Enforcement units stopped. Their heads turned toward the Praetor.
The Praetor's stone tablet body cracked.
"That's... not possible. The Enforcement Division cannot be purchased."
"I just did."
"The price is —"
"The price was half a billion. Which I have. Because my balance is infinite."
She pocketed the card.

"The Enforcement Division now works for me. Which means the Primordial Council no longer has an army."
She smiled.

"So. About that judgment."
\n---\n
Roxanne read the declaration on her card's display while sipping her morning latte.
"Rogue actor. That's dramatic."
"It's the standard terminology for 'we're about to send an army,'" Silas said.
She finished her latte. Set down the cup.
"Let them send it."
The army arrived at noon.

Twelve Enforcement units. A Praetor — a being of solidified law, its body made of inscribed stone tablets, its voice the sound of a gavel striking marble. Behind them, three War-spawned constructs: beasts of molten bronze and brass, each the size of a small house.
The campus went silent. Everyone felt it. The weight of the Enforcement Division's presence pressed down like a physical force.
Roxanne walked out to meet them. Silas beside her. The Executioner behind her. The two Enforcement units she'd purchased flanking her — former Council enforcers, now her employees.

"Roxanne Vance." The Praetor's voice echoed off the buildings. "By order of the Primordial Council, you are charged with: unauthorized use of a Primordial artifact, interference with Nether-Grid economic operations, and incitement of debtors. Surrender the card and submit to judgment."
Roxanne looked at the twelve Enforcement units. The three war beasts. The towering figure of the Praetor.
"I have a counter-offer."
"No further negotiations are authorized."
"I wasn't negotiating. I was informing."
She raised the card.
"DISPLAY: ENFORCEMENT DIVISION — COMPLETE INVENTORY BUYOUT."
The card pulsed. Numbers scrolled across the translucent screen — the price of every Enforcement unit, the war beasts, even the Praetor itself.
*TOTAL: 500,000,000 OBOLS.*
"Purchase."
*APPROVED.*
The twelve Enforcement units stopped. Their heads turned toward the Praetor.
The war beasts sat down.
The Praetor's stone tablet body cracked — a hairline fracture running from its shoulder to its hip.
"That's... not possible," it said.
"I just did it."
"The Enforcement Division cannot be purchased. It is not an asset."
"Everything has a price. The card shows me the price. I paid it."
She pocketed the card.
"Now. The Enforcement Division works for me. Which means the Primordial Council no longer has an army."
The Praetor was silent for a long, grinding moment.
"This is unprecedented."
"So is an art student holding an infinite black card. We're both breaking new ground today."
She smiled.
"Now. Let's talk about that judgment."
The Praetor's stone tablets cracked further. A fracture ran up its arm, across its shoulder, into its chest.
"You cannot buy the Enforcement Division. It is a regulatory body, not an asset."
"I just bought twelve of its members. The Division's contract terms allow unit-by-unit purchase. I checked."
"The Division's terms are classified."
"They were. The card unclassified them."
The Praetor was silent. Cracks spread across its form like a breaking dam.
"This is... an unprecedented violation of protocol."
"Write a report about it. I'm sure someone will read it eventually."
She turned to the twelve Enforcement units.
"New orders: return to your posts. Protect this campus and its inhabitants. If the Primordial Council sends anyone else, detain them."
The units nodded. One of them — the same one that had saluted earlier — raised its hand again.
"Yes, ma'am."
It spoke. The Enforcement Division could speak.
Roxanne blinked.
"You can talk?"
"We prefer not to. It undermines the intimidating aesthetic."
She stared at it.
"I just bought twelve enforcers who care about their aesthetic."
"We have standards, ma'am."
"Five hundred million Obols is a lot of money," Silas said, watching the Enforcement units accept their new contract. "Even for an infinite card."
"It's a number on a screen. Nothing more."
"You spent what would have been the GDP of a small country in five seconds."
"A small country's GDP was imaginary numbers on spreadsheets. This bought twelve Enforcement units and broke a Primordial Council army. That's real value."
The Praetor had retreated. Its stone tablet body had cracked further during the confrontation, and it had withdrawn through a portal, muttering about "unprecedented violations of protocol."
"The Council will escalate," Silas said.
"Let them. I have an army now."
"An army you just bought. They need time to integrate."
"How much time?"
"Days. Maybe weeks. Soldiers need to trust their commander."
"Then I'll earn their trust."
